explanation: This requirement implies that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true, possibly at the same time or after. In LTL, 'confirmation' often means that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must eventually occur afterwards (possibly immediately).

To say 'prop_0 shall confirm the prop_1 works' is interpreted as: whenever prop_0 occurs, it is in a situation where prop_1 holds (works). This means that globally, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 is also true.

The statement requires that 'prop_0' holds during the self-test procedure, with no temporal constraints specified. Assuming the self-test refers to the entire (current) temporal scope, we use globally.
Whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This can be expressed by requiring that globally, prop_0 implies prop_1.
The statement 'The prop_0 shall prop_1' is interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should also hold. In LTL, this is commonly expressed as 'globally (prop_0 implies prop_1)'.

The requirement suggests that whenever prop_0 is true, it must be the case that prop_1 works (i.e., is true as well). This is most directly represented as a global implication: globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 also holds.

To translate 'The prop_0 shall confirm the prop_1 works,' we assume that whenever prop_0 is true, it should only be because prop_1 is (or was) already true. We encode this by stating that globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds. This ensures that prop_0 can only hold in situations where prop_1 is already holding, i.e., when prop_1 'works.'

The statement means that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 should also occur (either at the same moment or eventually, depending on interpretation). In strict confirmation semantics, we typically require that every time prop_0 happens, eventually prop_1 happens. In LTL, this is represented as globally (if prop_0, then eventually prop_1).
The requirement says that whenever 'prop_0' occurs, 'prop_1' must also occur at that point. This means it is always the case that if 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' is true.

Assuming 'shall' represents an obligation that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 should hold (i.e., safety property), this can be captured by globally stating that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold.
